coredata.dc:title: Luminescent properties of fluorescent colour water-based ink films
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Gajadhur, Marta
coredata.dc:description: The article presents the luminance results of printouts for eight fluorescent inks intended for various applications, e.g., securing documents and packages, safety signs, or decorative effects. The research involves inks with 30% pigment concentration. The novelty is to show the luminescence depending on the ink film thickness and colour properties. The range of printout ink layer thicknesses is from 0 to 100 μm. The article presents scanning electron images and energy dispersive analysis of tested pigments, spectrofluorometric measures of lifetime, quantum yield, and Stokes shift. It also shows the rheological properties of pigment powder and inks and the optical properties, i.e., colour parameters, transparency, gloss, luminescence intensity, and the fluorescence expressed by the ΔCh, Δb∗, and ΔL∗ parameters. The surface topography and morphology, grain size, pigment shoreline, and roughness of the particles influence the fluorescent effect. The elemental composition, the b∗ colour parameter value in the CIELAB space, and lightness also affect the fluorescence. Pigments with a small grain and a smoother surface, such as chrome, orange, and yellow, shine much better than those with a more significant pigment grain and a rougher structure, such as violet and blue. The topography of pigments also affects their rheological properties and ink rheology. An increase in the ink layer thickness resulted in a decrease in its gloss and transparency values. However, the gloss and transparency do not affect the level of fluorescence, which increases with the ink film thickness to a certain maximum specified for a given ink colour. The equations in the article enable the fluorescent ink users to predict and adjust the appropriate thickness of its layer to obtain desired glowing effects.

head.ce:title: Luminescent properties of fluorescent colour water-based ink films

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The article presents the luminance results of printouts for eight fluorescent inks intended for various applications, e.g., securing documents and packages, safety signs, or decorative effects. The research involves inks with 30% pigment concentration. The novelty is to show the luminescence depending on the ink film thickness and colour properties. The range of printout ink layer thicknesses is from 0 to 100 μm. The article presents scanning electron images and energy dispersive analysis of tested pigments, spectrofluorometric measures of lifetime, quantum yield, and Stokes shift. It also shows the rheological properties of pigment powder and inks and the optical properties, i.e., colour parameters, transparency, gloss, luminescence intensity, and the fluorescence expressed by the ΔCh, Δb∗, and ΔL∗ parameters. The surface topography and morphology, grain size, pigment shoreline, and roughness of the particles influence the fluorescent effect. The elemental composition, the b∗ colour parameter value in the CIELAB space, and lightness also affect the fluorescence. Pigments with a small grain and a smoother surface, such as chrome, orange, and yellow, shine much better than those with a more significant pigment grain and a rougher structure, such as violet and blue. The topography of pigments also affects their rheological properties and ink rheology. An increase in the ink layer thickness resulted in a decrease in its gloss and transparency values. However, the gloss and transparency do not affect the level of fluorescence, which increases with the ink film thickness to a certain maximum specified for a given ink colour. The equations in the article enable the fluorescent ink users to predict and adjust the appropriate thickness of its layer to obtain desired glowing effects.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The raw/processed data required to reproduce these findings cannot be shared as the data also forms part of an ongoing study.

ce:section.ce:para: In many areas of industry, including the production of security prints, transport and road safety [1,2], road signs, construction and architecture [2], decorative business, occupational health and safety markings [2], security and evacuation systems [2], uniformed services, imaging microscopy [3], textile and beauty industry [4–6], entertainment, advertising, etc., we find fluorescent liquid and pigment application. That proves the very high importance of the topic discussed in this article.

The application properties of fluorescent materials are so excellent that they are now even used in environmental protection. Various types of multi-channel fluorescent paper-based microfluidic chips enable the detection of elements such as lead, arsenic, cadmium, and mercury in water and food products [7–9]. Fluorescent sensors from carbon dots (CQD) can be obtained from natural sources, e.g., Coleus leaves (Coleus zeylanicus), Sida cordifolia, mushrooms (Cordyceps militaris). They detect pollutants, metal ions, antibiotics, alcohol, and many other substances. They are also becoming a new source of creating physically and chemically stable fluorescent inks that encrypt or store information [8–10]. Various types of multi-channel fluorescent paper-based microfluidic chips enable the detection of elements such as lead, arsenic, cadmium, and mercury in food products [7]. Other articles [8–10] also discuss the problem of fluorescent penetrating pigments intended for document security. New synthesized fluorescent inks change colour under UV irradiation [11], and fluorescent liquids find application in high-quality OLED displays [8].

Production of coloured fluorescent pigments increasingly uses graphene or carbon quantum dots doped with various elements, e.g., nitrogen (N-GQD – Nitrogen-doped Graphene Quantum Dots, N-CQD – Nitrogen-doped Carbon Quantum Dots), sulfur (S-GQD – Sulfur-doped Graphene Quantum Dots, S-CQD – Sulfur-doped Carbon Quantum Dots) [12,13], phosphorus [14] or fluorine (F-GQD – Fluorine-doped Graphene Quantum Dots, F-CQD – Fluorine-doped Carbon Quantum Dots) [15]. Distinguishing the element doping concentration, pH, or binder polarity led to different lengths of the pigment light wave emitted, from blue to yellow-green [15]. Increasing the solvent polarity shifts the material emission towards longer wavelengths. Quantum dots doped with various elements are relatively new materials, and their popularity is constantly growing, as evidenced by the multitude of articles on this subject in prestigious international journals. These materials show more photostability and exceptional fluorescence brightness than ordinary fluorescent materials. Many industries, such as molecular sensors [16], security prints [12,14,15,17], luminescent diodes, optical displays [18], information security [12,17], biomedical applications [19], and others, use them. In addition to a wide range of applications, fluorescent quantum dots are biocompatible, ecologically produced and of low toxicity [18].

Fluorescent inks are available for all printing techniques from offset [20,21], screen, flexography [22,23], gravure printing, electrophotographic printing [24], or ink-jet [25,26], as well as 3D printing technology [27].

The issues related to the specific properties of pigments, such as fluorescence, conductivity, thermochromic, pearl effect, or other special-effect properties and their investigation methods, are the subject of interest of authors from different research centers [20,23,24,28–35].

Despite the extensive literature on fluorescent inks or fluorescent compounds and their applications, there is no information on how the ink film thickness or colour of the ink layer affects the intensity of the fluorescent effect; this article addresses this deficiency.

The research required.
−
eight fluorescent pigments: Iris Green SipFast XT3 series: yellow PFG 1600122, orange PFG 1600247, pink PFG 1600362, green PFG 1600723, blue PFG 1600551, magenta PFG 1600373, and Finco: chrome 3113, violet 3119; pigment density 1.36 g cm^-3, ph 7–8, decomposition temperature 250 °C [36], all pigments bought from Decorpigment, Poland [37] (Fig. 1 ), chrome, yellow, green, magenta – Texas Red, sulforhodamine 101 acid chloride xanthene derivatives fluorophores, orange, pink, blue, violet – sulforhodamine 101 xanthene derivatives fluorophores,
−
water-base flexographic binder Hydro-Shield GA (60HX581925) from hubergroup, Poland,
−
Munken Pure paper base of grammage 200 g m-2, uncoated creamy without optical brighteners from Arctic Paper, Poland,
−
Radwag AS 220/X analytical balance (measurement accuracy: ±0.0001 g),
−
Anton Paar Visco QC 300 R for rheological properties evaluation (±0,2%), CC12/D18 spindle,
−
RK Print K bars No. 0–8 (characteristic of printing bars is in Table 1 ),
−
RK Print K control coater,
−
eXact X-rite spectrophotometer for CIELab evaluation (measurement accuracy: ±0.01),
−
Spectro Eye X-rite spectrophotometer for paper whiteness, brightness, and transparency evaluation (measurement accuracy: ±0.01),
−
X-Rite Data Catcher software for collecting the colorimetric data of obtained prints,
−
Sekonic Dual Master L-558 exposure meter for luminance evaluation (measuring range ambient ISO 100: 2÷22.9 eV, measurement accuracy: ±0.1 eV),
−
UV lamp (wavelength 366 nm),
−
KRÜSS drop shape analyzer – DSA 30 with Advance Drop Shape Software for ink surface tension measurements,
−
ZEISS Ultra Plus Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM) with Bruker Quantax 400 and Esprit software EDS microanalysis,
−
FS5 Edinburgh Instruments Spectrofluorometer, with SC-10 Front Face Sample Holder, SC30 Integrating Sphere, EPLED 340 nm laser beam and Fluoracle software,
−
Dino-Lite AM4113T-FV2W digital microscope with Dino Capture 2.0 software (magnification up to 200x, LED white and LED UV ∼375 nm),
−
Picogloss 503 gloss meter for measuring the gloss of the printed surface (±0.1),
−
HP Color LaserJet CM 1312 MFP for printing black patches for transparency test.

Tests used the eight fluorescent pigments covering the visible radiation spectrum from short to long wavelength. Fluorescent inks contained the binder and 30% of the appropriate pigment. The chrome ink was also with 5% and 15% pigment content. An analytical balance allowed the weighing of the binder and pigments. The total amount of each ink was 30 mL. The microscopic assessment was in UV and white LED irradiation at 200× magnification using a Dino-Lite microscope and Dino Capture 2.0 software. The EDS method led to the pigment composition evaluation. Zeiss scanning microscope took images at 5000× and 10,000× magnification.

FS5 Edinburgh Instruments Spectrofluorometer tested the pigment fluorescent values, such as lifetimes, Stokes shifts, and quantum yields. The front-face collection mode measured the emission spectra of powder samples. The integrating sphere measured the quantum yields of powder samples according to the known procedure [39]. Excitation of solid samples was at the wavelength corresponding to the absorption maximum observed in the solid state. The Time-Correlated Single Photon Counting (TCSPC) system with a picosecond pulsed 340 nm EPLED source measured fluorescent lifetime. The final determination of fluorescence lifetimes included the Instrument Response Function (IRF).

Anton Paar Visco QC 300 R device assessed the rheological properties. Tests were in an air-conditioned room under constant conditions of 20 °C temperature and 50% relative humidity. Constant 84 s−1 and accelerated from 0 to 10 s−1 shear rate led to a rheology assessment of pigments and inks. The results present the mean of shear stress and viscosity based on three measurements for each sample. The surface tension assessment of the prepared ink and binder used the pendant drop method, the Krüss Advance software, and ten steps for each ink and binder. This method required a needle with a diameter of approx. 1.8 μm. The rod applicators numbered 0–8 with different wire diameters led to obtaining the printouts with varying ink layer thicknesses on uncoated creamy paper without optical brighteners. The print area was 10 × 5 cm^2 (h x w). The data given by the RK Print K-bar manufacturer made it possible to estimate the value of ink film thickness Table 1. The evaluation of the samples optical properties was possible approximately 24 h after printing.

Spectrophotometer eXact x-Rite and 2° colorimetric observer in M0 (illuminant A), M1 (in daylight, illuminant D50), and M2 (with UV cut, illuminant A) standards led to colorimetric measurement. The CIELAB ΔCh, Δb*, and ΔL* calculations based on Eq. (1) [23,40] and Eq. (2). ΔCh and Δb* values enable determining the fluorescence level of the samples. The evaluation of luminous intensity with the Sekonic Dual Master meter was in the darkroom using a UV lamp. The Sekonic light meter gave the values of ink luminance intensity in electronvolts. Equation (3) converted them to luxs. Picogloss 503 gloss meter measured the surface gloss in 20°, 60°, and 85° geometry. Spectro Eye Gretag Macbeth spectrophotometer measured the ISO and CIE whiteness substrate parameters using C and D65 illuminant and 2 and 10° colorimetric observer.

Spectro Eye spectrophotometer also measured the reflection coefficients of the fluorescent eight inks applied on the white paper and printed with HP Color LaserJet black full-tone coverage patches. Equation (4) [41] led to transparency calculation for eight fluorescent inks in the wide range of ink film thickness achieved with different printing rods.

Fig. 2 presents an exemplary transparency test for yellow and all tested fluorescent inks in white and UV irradiation.

Tables 2 and 4
present the properties of the paper tested in the research. It was creamy, uncoated paper without optical brighteners. Measurements in M1 and M2 standards enabled the collection of CIELAB colorimetric values. Equations (1) and (2) led to the computation of ΔCh and Δb* parameters. The small values of these parameters (Table 3) prove that there were no optical brighteners in the paper used in the research. Illuminant C and D65, 2 and 10° colorimetric observers selection provided paper whiteness and brightness value measurements. Values of paper whiteness CIE below 100 and brightness ISO of about 100 also indicate the lack of optical brighteners in the printing substrate.
Table 5 shows maximum excitation and emission wavelength, Stokes shifts calculated on the difference between maximum emission and excitation wavelength values and quantum yields of tested pigments.

Analysing wavelengths using a calculator enabling colour visualization for a specific wavelength [42], we see that the maximum excitation of the chrome pigment occurs in green light, yellow in blue light, orange and pink in yellow-green (lemon) light, green in blue light, blue under violet light, magenta under yellow light, violet under near UV light. Therefore, we can see a similarity in the wavelengths of maximum excitation and maximum emission for yellow and green pigments and another similarity for orange, pink, and magenta pigments.

Fig. 3 presents graphically the maximum emission of eight fluorescent pigments used in the research. Analysing the wavelengths at which the maximum emission occurs, we see that for chrome, orange, pink, and magenta pigments, it is an orange colour differing in the intensity of colour saturation. For green, it is green; for blue, it is blue; and for violet, the emission occurs in two ranges, in the range of blue and red, but for the red one, it is more intense.

Fig. 4 a shows a graphical interpretation of the Stokes shift value of eight pigments. We see the smallest value for orange and green pigments and the biggest for violet and yellow. In the case of other pigments, this value remains at a similar level between 40 and 50 nm.

Four tested pigments (chrome, yellow, orange, and pink) have higher quantum yields than the others, resulting in higher luminescence intensity. Fig. 4b shows a graphical interpretation of these data. The quantum yield of the chrome pigment is the highest and is about 94%; for yellow, it is slightly lower, about 93%; for orange and pink, it is about 80%; for green and magenta, it is half as much, approximately 40%. The blue and violet pigment quantum yield is about 20%, four times as small as the four presented most luminous pigments.

Table 6 shows the lifetimes of pigments. There are two lifetimes in some cases (yellow, orange, green, blue, and violet), so the table gives the average. Pink, chrome, and orange pigments have the most extended lifetimes (about five ns), while violet and blue have the shortest (about one ns). For magenta and yellow, the lifetime is about four ns, and for green, it is about three ns.

Fig. 5 presents scanning electron micrographs with 1,000× magnification of the fluorescent pigments used in the research. Fig. 5a presents chrome pigment, Fig. 5b yellow pigment, Fig. 5c orange pigment, Fig. 5d pink pigment, Fig. 5e green pigment, Fig. 5f blue pigment, Fig. 5g magenta pigment, Fig. 5h violet pigment. The images show selected particle sizes. Chrome and yellow pigments (Fig. 5a and b) have the smallest pigment particles, followed by orange and pink (Fig. 5d and e). The largest amount of big pigment particles are for blue (Fig. 5f) and violet (Fig. 5h). The largest observed particle sizes, approx. 26 μm, are for the blue color (Fig. 5f); in the remaining cases, the largest particles have dimensions of approx. 15 μm (Fig. 5a, Fig. 5c–h), only for the yellow pigment, the grains do not exceed 13 μm (Fig. 5b). Chrome and orange pigments (Fig. 5a and c) show a lower tendency to agglomerate and aggregate into clusters, possibly contributing to more even pigment distribution in the ink.

Fig. 6 presents scanning electron micrographs with 10,000× magnification of the fluorescent pigments used in the research. Fig. 6a presents chrome pigment, Fig. 6b yellow pigment, Fig. 6c orange pigment, Fig. 6d pink pigment, Fig. 6e green pigment, Fig. 6f blue pigment, Fig. 6g magenta pigment, Fig. 6h violet pigment. The drawings Fig. 6a–h show the dimensions of the smallest and largest particles of a given pigment visible on these figures. The figures Fig. 6a–c show that smaller particles dominate larger ones in chrome, yellow, and orange pigments. For the rest Fig. 6d–h, the dominance of large-sized particles is visible. The pigments surface of the chrome Fig. 6a, yellow Fig. 6b, and orange Fig. 6c larger particles are smoother than the other pigments. The particle sizes for chrome, yellow, and orange pigments, Fig. 6a–c, start from 0.16 to 0.27 μm, while for the remaining pigments, Fig. 6d–h, the grain sizes start from 0.35 μm.

Fig. 7 presents microscope images with 200× magnification of the fluorescent pigments used in the research under white LED and UV irradiation. Fig. 7a presents chrome pigment under white LED irradiation, and Fig. 7b shows the same pigment under UV irradiation. Fig. 7c gives yellow pigment under white LED irradiation, and Fig. 7d the same pigment under UV irradiation. Fig. 7e presents orange pigment under white LED irradiation, and Fig. 7f shows orange pigment under UV irradiation. Fig. 7g gives pink pigment under white LED irradiation, and Fig. 7h the same pigment under UV irradiation. Fig. 7i illustrates green pigment under white LED irradiation and Fig. 7j green pigment under UV irradiation. Fig. 7k presents blue pigment under white LED irradiation, and Fig. 7l shows blue pigment under UV irradiation. Fig. 7m shows magenta pigment under white LED irradiation, and Fig. 7n illustrates magenta pigment under UV irradiation. Fig. 7o presents violet pigment under white LED irradiation and Fig. 7p presents the same pigment under UV irradiation.

Four pigments, chrome, orange, green, and blue, show colours in UV irradiation corresponding to those observed in white LED irradiation (Fig. 7a and b–e and f–i and j–k and l). Four pigments: yellow (Fig. 7c and d), pink (Fig. 7g and h), magenta (Fig. 7m and n) and violet (Fig. 7o and p) look different in UV than in white LED irradiation. The yellow pigment (Fig. 7c) turns olive in white LED irradiation, and in UV irradiation, Fig. 7d turns green. Pigments pink (Fig. 7g and h), magenta (Fig. 7m and n), and violet (Fig. 7o and p) turn orange or even red under UV irradiation (Fig. 7h–n, Fig. 7p). The saturation of the observed orange colour in three photos (Fig. 7h–n, Fig. 7p) is lower for the pink pigment (Fig. 7h), higher for the magenta (Fig. 7n) and redder for the violet pigment (Fig. 7p). The observed colour in the images Fig. 7a–p coincide with the emission spectra of the eight tested pigments (Fig. 3) and the wavelength at which the maximum emission occurs (Table 5). The wavelength analysis with a calculator enabling colour visualization [42] shows that the colours coincide with the microscopic observations for the discussed photos Fig. 7a–p.

The observations show that the pink (Fig. 7g and h), magenta (Fig. 7m and n), and violet pigment (Fig. 7o and p) appear orange in UV irradiation. The yellow pigment in UV irradiation generates a colour close to green (Fig. 7d).
Fig. 8 and Table 7 present chemical compound analysis EDS (Energy Dispersive Spectroscopy) of the eight fluorescent pigments used in the research.

Fig. 9 presents a chart indicating the composition of the eight fluorescence pigments. The elemental composition of the six investigated pigments is similar to each other. Only the magenta and violet pigments contain more carbon and sulfur than the others and do not contain nitrogen. Therefore, six pigments have carbon dots doped with nitrogen and sulfur, significantly affecting the luminance described in the next part of the article. Many articles proved that doping carbon dots with nitrogen or sulfur significantly affects the quantum yield of fluorescence [3,12,13].

Chrome and yellow pigments contain chlorine, which also influence the glow effect. Orange, pink, blue, and violet pigments do not contain chlorine. The remaining four pigments (chrome, yellow, green, and magenta) contain small amounts of halogens that influence the luminescence effect of these pigments. Literature reports show that chlorinated graphene quantum dots (Cl-GQDs) show better optoelectronic properties and show a shift towards blue waves [43]; doping with chlorine also results in higher electronegativity, which in turn has a significant impact on the luminescence efficiency, as confirmed in the articles [43–45].

Taking into account the elemental composition, we can deduce that chrome, yellow, green, and magenta pigments belong to the group of fluorophores derived from xanthene (sulforhodamine 101 acid chloride, Texas Red) because they contain carbon, nitrogen, sulfur, oxygen, and chlorine. Orange, pink, blue, and violet pigments also belong to sulforhodamine 101, a group of fluorophores that are derivatives of xanthene and contain carbon, nitrogen, sulfur, and oxygen and do not contain chlorine.

Table 8 presents the viscosity and shear stress of eight fluorescent pigments used in the research. Three consecutive measurements of the same pigment sample prepared in a powder loose form show how it behaves during the spindle work, whether they are densified or stays loose. The measurements are at a constant shear rate of 84 s−1. Fig. 10 presents the graphical interpretation of rheological properties, such as mean shear stress and mean viscosity measured at 84 s−1 shear rate.

Table 8 shows that shear weakens most pigments (chrome, yellow, blue, and violet). Three, i.e., orange, pink, and magenta, are densified by shearing. As the spindle operates, the powder is compacted, and firm connections are between its grains at the shoreline interface. The viscosity of the remaining pigments, chrome, yellow, blue, and violet, decreases with subsequent measurements, which means that as the spindle runs, the powder structure loosens. There are no clusters of pigment particles; thus, the pigment has a greater tendency to disperse evenly in other media. There is also a minor surface contact between the particles at the grain boundary and, therefore, a lower friction force, which applies to chrome and yellow pigments. In the case of the yellow pigment, the decrease in viscosity with subsequent measurements is the most visible of all the pigments tested. Chrome, yellow, and orange show lower viscosity and shear stress than the others (Fig. 10a). The chrome shows the smallest values of all tested. The observed regularities may be due to the pigment grain size or the texture softness. Lower viscous pigments exhibit lower shear stress (Fig. 10b) and thus reduced friction force at the interface between the pigment particles. Minor shear stress may be due to less boundary roughness. A smoother pigment surface may cause directional reflection, thus a greater ink luminosity. SEM micrographs at 10,000 magnification also confirm this conclusion about surface topography. The next part of the article shows that the chrome pigment glows the most. Compared to the others, the green pigment has the highest viscosity and shear stress, which indicates the roughest boundary line of all tested in this work. SEM microscopic photos also confirm this (Fig. 6).

Table 9 , Fig. 11 a and b, Fig. 12 a, and Fig. 12b present the rheological properties of a binder and fluorescent inks prepared from the binder with 30% fluorescent pigment content (chrome, yellow, orange, pink, green, blue, magenta, violet). Fig. 11a presents the viscosity and Fig. 11b shear stress under increasing from 0 to 10 s−1 share rate of eight fluorescent inks with 30% fluorescent pigment added to the binder. Fig. 12a presents the viscosity and Fig. 12b gives the share stress of the same inks tested under a constant 84 s−1 share rate. Samples were in a liquid form.

Based on the results, the pink fluorescent ink has the highest viscosity (approx. 150 mPa s). Inks: green, blue, magenta, and violet show viscosity at a similar level (approx. 140 mPa s). For all inks, the viscosity is almost 2–3 times as high as the viscosity of the binder. The shear stress is also approximately 2–3 times as high as the binder. The shear stress of the green ink is eight times as high as the binder. Higher viscosity values (Fig. 11a) by several to a dozen or so mPa s and much lower shear stress (Fig. 11b) from 5 to 10 Pa are for a lower shear rate (10 s-1) than higher (84 s-1, Fig. 12a Fig. 12b) share rate for all tested inks. The lowest viscosity value of the tested inks (Fig. 12a) and the lowest shear stress (Fig. 12b) is for the orange, chrome, and yellow colours, which show the highest luminous intensity in the next part of this article. The regularities are similar to the rheology results of the pigments without a binder (Fig. 10a and b). A much higher shear stress value is for the green (Fig. 11b) at a lower shear rate. It confirms the greater boundary roughness of the particles of this pigment and the lower roughness of the orange, chrome, and yellow, thus a lower friction coefficient at the binder-pigment interface. Adding 30% pigment to the binder increases its viscosity by about 2.5 times. Considerations on the morphology of the pigment and the viscosity of the ink were also the subject of other authors [46,47].

Table 10 and Fig. 13 a present a surface tension of a binder and fluorescent inks prepared with 30% pigment concentration. The average surface tension value calculation was on ten measurements. Fig. 13b shows the ink exemplary drop shape analysis and surface tension value with 30% violet fluorescent pigment content.

The lowest surface tension of the inks was determined for the yellow, chrome, and orange, containing 30% of the pigment. Others show a surface tension close to the binder, especially pink, blue, and violet. The green and magenta surface tension is slightly smaller. The results are consistent with the earlier discussion regarding the smoothness of the boundary line of individual pigment particles. The lowest viscosity and shear stress of yellow, chrome, and orange pigments, measured both without and with the binder is due to the smoothest particle surface and the lowest coefficient of friction. The surface tension of these three pigments is much lower than the others. Therefore, they need less energy per unit area and, thus, less work to increase the contact area by that unit. The remaining pigments have a rougher boundary surface at the solid-binder interface. Therefore, they need more energy, thus a higher surface tension to increase the surface area per surface unit. Considerations of the relationship between surface tension and surface roughness appear in many publications [23,48–50].

Fig. 14 presents 85° gloss value changes of different film thicknesses of the ink with 5, 15, and 30% chrome fluorescent pigment addition and of the binder. Fig. 14a presents the bar chart and Fig. 14b a linear function interpretation.

In this case, the mean value of the determination coefficient is R2 = 0.82.

Limiting the gloss 85° results of three chrome fluorescent pigment concentrations by the boundary curves of the linear functions is possible. The highest linear function curve is for ink with 5% chrome pigment concentration (Eq. (6)). The lowest linear approximation (Eq. (7)) is for ink with a 30% pigment content. Eq. Therefore, we observe a reduction of this area by 454 μm due to the pigment added to the binder.

Fig. 15 presents the 60° gloss of binder and inks with 30% fluorescent pigment content in the function of ink film thickness. Fig. 15a shows the bar chart for all tested ink film thicknesses. Fig. 15b presents the bar chart of 100 μm maximum ink film thickness. Based on the charts, we observe a significant decrease in the layer gloss for all the tested inks due to adding 30% pigment. The chrome and green pigments have the highest gloss value. However, the gloss values measured at an angle of 60° are low in all cases.

Fig. 16 presents transparency changes of different film thicknesses of the ink with 5, 15, and 30% chrome fluorescent pigment addition and of the binder. Fig. 16a presents the bar chart of transparency changes. A linear function can describe these changes presented in Fig. 16b with a range of coefficients in Eq. (11). The chrome fluorescent ink had the best luminous intensity presented in the following sections of this article, which is why the results are additionally for this ink transparency of different pigment content.

The increase in the thickness of the ink layer prepared only on the binder does not affect the layer transparency changes. The transparency in the layer range from 0 to 100 μm is approx. 95%, and the differences do not exceed 1.8%. The 5% chrome fluorescent pigment content causes layer transparency changes up to 7.6%. The average transparency value, in this case, is 91%. For 15% chrome pigment content, the average value of layer transparency is 87%, and transparency changes in the 0–100 μm layer thickness are 15%. For 30% pigment content, the average transparency of the layer is 84%, and the transparency fluctuations do not exceed 8.5%.

The area limited by the boundary lines for 5% chrome and 30% pigment content is about 7 μm. To conclude, due to the chrome fluorescent pigment added to the binder, the value of the transparency area decreases by approximately 4 μm. Table 11 presents the transparency research results. The same way of deduction as in the case of gloss led to these calculations. To sum up, the ink pigment content influence on the layer transparency is small.

Fig. 17 presents the transparency of binder and inks with 30% fluorescent pigment content in the function of ink film thickness both for the bar chart (Fig. 17a) and linear function presentation (Fig. 17b).

Analyzing the transparency of eight fluorescent inks with 30% pigment content, we see that the highest transparency in the layer thickness range from 0 to 100 μm is for yellow and the lowest for violet. A linear function with the average value of the determination coefficient of R2 = 0.97 and the values of the linear coefficients F1 and F2 given in Eq. (12) can describe the dependencies for all inks.

Limiting the transparency value of eight fluorescent pigments is possible by the boundary curves of the linear functions. The highest linear function curve is for ink with 30% yellow fluorescent pigment. The lowest linear function is for the ink with 30% violet pigment. This area is called the area normal to the OX axis, limited by the two curves and the lines parallel to the OY axis crossing through the values 0 and 100. The calculations for integrals mark the thickness of the ink layer (d) with the letter (x) to simplify the notation.

The delta chromaticity parameter ΔCh measured in two standards, i.e., M1 – with UV irradiation and M2 – without UV irradiation, spectrophotometrically determine the fluorescence level. This part presents the fluorescence results.

Fig. 19 shows delta chromaticity (ΔCh) changes of different ink film thicknesses with 5, 15, and 30% chrome fluorescent pigment addition. Fig. 19a presents a bar chart diagram, and Fig. 19b shows the linear data interpretation.

Similarly to the gloss and transparency case, a linear equation can describe the dependences of delta chromaticity (ΔCh) and pigment concentration on ink film thickness. The coefficient values range is in Eq. (14) . The results can limit the linear function coefficient ranges calculated based on the lowest and highest values obtained for three fluorescent pigment concentrations (5, 15, and 30%). In this case, the mean value of the determination coefficient is R2 = 0.76.

The highest linear function curve is for ink with 30% chrome pigment concentration Eq. (15). The lowest linear function (Eq. (16)) is for ink with a 5% pigment content. Eq. (17) calculates the area bounded by the boundary curves Eq. (15) and Eq. (16) based on the difference of integrals on the ink layer thickness from 0 to 100 μm is 907 μm.

The analysis of the PΔCh fluorescence area changes due to the ink layer thickness from 0 to 100 μm separately for each chrome pigment concentration shows the most significant variations in the fluorescence level for 5% chrome pigment content and the smallest for 15%. The data in Table 13 refers to a triangle area bounded by a straight line approximating the test results for a given pigment concentration and a straight line parallel to the OX axis intersecting it at the zero point ink layer thickness.

Fig. 20 shows fluorescence changes described by the ΔCh parameter for eight fluorescent inks tested in the work. Fig. 20a shows the bar chart and Fig. 20b the linear data interpretation. The results refer to the 30% pigment content. The surface area of PΔCh fluorescence in the ink layer thickness ranges from 0 to 100 μm, limited by extreme curves for chrome and violet ink, is 1949 μm. Changes in the value of the ΔCh parameter in the ink film thickness range for most fluorescent inks are about 12 units. More minor changes in fluorescence caused by the layer thickness are for the green pigment of about 6.4 ΔCh units, magenta 5.7 ΔCh units, blue – about 2.7 ΔCh units, or yellow and violet – approx. 2 ΔCh units.
Determining the level of fluorescence using the ΔCh parameter is indicative. Light meter measurements give more precise results.

The highest fluorescence values expressed by the ΔCh parameter are for chrome, yellow, and orange pigments, and the lowest for violet and blue. Thus, a given colour lightness level affects its luminance intensity.

Fig. 21 shows changes in fluorescence expressed by the Δb* parameter difference measured in M1 and M2 standards for eight fluorescent inks tested in the work. Fig. 21a presents the bar chart visualization and Fig. 21b the linear function interpretation. The published results refer to the highest pigment percentage in the ink, which is 30%. The surface area of fluorescence PΔb* in the ink film thickness ranges from 0 to 100 μm, limited by extreme curves for chrome pigment and violet pigment, is 1929 μm. The surface area of fluorescence changes calculated based on the Δb* parameter is approx. 18.5 times as big for chrome pigment as for violet. The average change in the value of the Δb* parameter of all fluorescent inks due to the ink film thickness is about 8 Δb* units. The lower the luminosity level, the smaller the changes in the fluorescence level. The highest fluorescence values expressed by the Δb* parameter are for chrome and orange pigments and the lowest for violet and blue.

Fig. 22 shows changes in fluorescence expressed by the ΔL* parameter difference measured in M1 and M2 standards for eight fluorescent inks tested in the work. Fig. 22a presents the bar chart and Fig. 22b the linear data interpretation. The published results refer to the highest pigment percentage in the ink, which is 30%. The surface area of fluorescence PΔL* in the ink film thickness ranges from 0 to 100 μm, limited by extreme curves for chrome pigment and violet pigment, is 1216 μm. The surface area of fluorescence changes calculated based on the ΔL* parameter is approx. 13 times as big for chrome pigment as for violet. The average change in the value of the ΔL* parameter of all fluorescent inks due to the ink film thickness is about 3.5 ΔL* units. The highest fluorescence values expressed by the ΔL* parameter are for chrome and orange pigments and the lowest for violet and blue.

Fig. 23 presents the luminance of eight tested inks with 30% fluorescent pigment concentration on the ink film thickness and printing bar number. Fig. 23a presents summary data. Chart diversification is for bright (Fig. 23b) and less bright pigments (Fig. 23c). Colours with a higher b* value in the CIELAB Hunter system close to yellow shine brighter than colours with a lower b* parameter closer to blue.

Comparing the chrome pigment average fluorescence lumination intensity values with the others, it is approximately thirty times as big as violet, six times as magenta, five times as blue and green, three times as pink, twice as orange, and comparable to yellow.

The second-degree polynomial function, in most cases, can describe the lumination of the fluorescent inks measured with the light meter. We observe a certain maximum light intensity for a specific ink layer thickness. Table 14 shows these calculated values.

Compared to the others examined in this work, chrome, yellow, and orange printouts have the best luminous properties. Dark colours, such as violet, have the worst fluorescence. Besides the quantum yield, lifetimes, and elemental composition of a given pigment, the intensity of the glow depends on the structural and surface properties, grain size, and colour brightness.

Comparing the chrome printouts average fluorescence lumination intensity values with the others, it is approximately thirty times as big as violet and five times as big as blue. The quantum yield of the chrome pigment itself is about six times as big as the violet pigment.

Four tested pigments (chrome, yellow, orange, and pink) have higher quantum yields than the others, resulting in higher luminescence intensity. Pink and chrome pigments have the longest lifetimes, while violet and blue have the shortest.

Chrome and orange pigments show a lower tendency to agglomerate and aggregate into clusters, contributing to the more even ink solvation structure. They also have smaller particle sizes, whereas the blue and violet pigments are more significant than the others.

The pigment surface of chrome, yellow, and orange particles is smoother than that of other pigments, besides the better fluorescence, which influences the lower viscosity and shear stress. Chrome shows the most negligible viscosity and shear stress values of all tested due to the pigment grain size and the softness of texture. Lower viscous pigments exhibit lower shear stress and thus reduced friction force at the interface between the pigment particles. Minor shear stress is, among others, due to less boundary roughness. A smoother pigment surface causes directional reflection, thus a greater ink luminosity.

Analogous regularities are in the case of ink viscosity. Similarly, these three inks of the best fluorescence have the lowest viscosity value, shear stress, and surface tension. Therefore, they need less energy per unit area and, thus, less work to increase the contact area by that unit. The remaining pigments have a rougher boundary surface at the solid-binder interface. Therefore, they need more energy, thus a higher surface tension to increase the surface area per surface unit. Adding 30% pigment to the binder increases its viscosity by about 2.5 times.

The elemental composition of the six investigated pigments is similar to each other. Only the magenta and violet contain more carbon and sulfur than the others and do not contain nitrogen. Six pigments have carbon dots doped with nitrogen and sulfur, significantly affecting the luminance. Four pigments (chrome, yellow, green, and magenta) contain small amounts of halogens that influence the luminescence effect of these pigments.
We observed a significant decrease in the layer gloss for all the tested inks due to adding 30% pigment to the binder. The chrome has the highest gloss value.
The highest transparency of eight fluorescent inks is for yellow, and the lowest for violet. That also translates into the glowing properties of the ink layer.

The highest fluorescence values expressed by the ΔCh parameter are for chrome, yellow, and orange pigments and the lowest for violet and blue. Thus, a given colour lightness level affects its luminance intensity. The highest fluorescence measured using the Δb* and ΔL* parameters is for chrome and orange pigments. Therefore, colours with a higher b* value in the CIELAB Hunter system close to yellow shine brighter than with a lower b* parameter closer to blue. The fluorescence values based on ΔCh and Δb* assessments are similar. The values obtained for the ΔL* parameter are smaller.

The second-degree polynomial function, in most cases, can describe the lumination of the fluorescent inks measured with the light meter. We observe a certain maximum light intensity for a specific ink layer thickness. Increasing the thickness of the ink layer reduces the gloss and transparency values. Therefore, the gloss and transparency do not affect the level of fluorescence, which rises to a certain maximum with ink film thickness.

It is possible to arrange the tested inks fluorescence intensity according to the colours of the white light spectrum. Inks emitting short waves glow the least (violet ink), while long waves glow the most (chrome, orange and yellow ink). UV radiation of short waves with high frequency and energy causes the ink fluorescence. Orange pigments emitting long waves need less excitation energy than violet emitting short waves. Therefore, colours emitting long waves are more intensely excited than short waves with the same dose of energy.
